EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Mesonet wind sensor in Leupp reported a peak wind gust of 60 MPH.

CZ_NAME: WHITE MOUNTAINS OF GRAHAM AND GREENLEE COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought (D3) conditions continued across part of southeast Arizona during August, but the overall area was reduced due to monsoonal rainfall. Portions of Cochise, Greenlee, Pima and northern Graham counties improved into the severe or moderate drought categories by month's end.  Western Graham and southeast Pinal counties remained in the extreme drought category.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: By early August, extreme drought (D3) conditions were reduced to the severe (D2) category across the White Mountains of Graham and Greenlee counties and the northern Chiricahua Mountains as a result of significant late July and early August rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought (D3) conditions continued across far eastern Pinal and much of western Graham Counties during September, but much of south central Pinal County was reduced to the severe drought category by month's end.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought (D3) category persisted across far eastern Pinal and western Graham Counties through September. While some portions of the extreme drought area received up to 150 percent of normal rainfall during the month, a significant area near the Pinal/Graham County line received as little as 50 percent of normal rainfall in September. Levels at San Carlos Reservoir rose to about 6 percent of capacity by the end of September, the highest level since May. Soil moisture also improved, rising to around 70 percent of normal. However, ranchers continued to be affected by the depletion of rangeland forage vegetation.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter near Prescott reported 1.95 inches of rain in 12 hours with 0.92 inches of that falling in just one hour.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN NAVAJO COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong low pressure system continued to move through the Great Basin and Northern Rockies on June 17. This produced windy to very windy conditions over northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Winslow ASOS reported strong winds from around noon to mid afternoon. The peak wind gust was 72 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and embedded thunderstorms developed across south central Arizona during the early morning hours on September 8th, and as the morning progressed the showers intensified and became more and more widespread. Partially due to an infusion of tropical moisture from former hurricane Norbert, the storms produced intense rainfall with rainfall rates commonly in excess of 2 inches per hour and in some cases, in excess of 6 inches per hour for short periods of time. By the middle of the morning, most of the central deserts had received over two inches of rain, and many locations across the greater Phoenix metropolitan area picked up between 3 and 6 inches of rainfall. Phoenix Sky Harbor airport received 3.29 inches of rainfall with this event; the rain total set an all time record for any calendar date. The rainfall caused widespread flash flooding followed by flooding; numerous roads were closed. Several freeways were closed, including Interstate 10, as pumping stations failed and lakes several feet deep formed across portions of the freeways. Hundreds of cars were stranded across the greater Phoenix area, many buried to the top of their hoods in flood waters. Hundreds of homes and apartment complexes across the greater Phoenix area were flooded. Heavy rains damaged roofs across the valley, and collapsed the roof of a Fry's grocery store in Tempe. Clearly, the heavy rainfall led to a historic flooding event across south central Arizona on September 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread showers and embedded thunderstorms developed across the central and southeast portion of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the early morning hours on September 8th, and they persisted into the middle of the morning. The showers produced intense rainfall; gages indicated rain rates from 2 to nearly 6 inches per hour at times. Many locations across Tempe and Chandler received 2.5 to 5 inches of rain during the morning. According to an on-line article posted by the East Valley Tribune, at about 0800MST heavy rain caused the collapse of a roof at the Fry's grocery store located at the intersection of Baseline Road and McClintock Road. The Tempe Fire Department indicated that there were no injuries, and that the store would be closed until further notice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high pressure center over New Mexico and a trough of low pressure off the California Coast brought a very moist southerly flow over northern Arizona. This led to many thunderstorms that produced heavy rain and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter 3 miles north of Cleator reported just over three inches of rain and flooded washes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high pressure center over New Mexico and a trough of low pressure off the California Coast brought a very moist southerly flow over northern Arizona. This led to many thunderstorms that produced heavy rain and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated that it rained around three inches. They also reported rock slides and debris form heavy rains on Interstate 17 from mile marker 242 to 250 (from around Black Canyon City to around the Sunset Point Rest Area).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm hit the Bagdad area with damaging winds and heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding at the intersection Highways 96 and 97. The roads were closed due to the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm hit the Bagdad area with damaging winds and heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Co-Op observer at the Freeport McMoran Mine reported a peak wind gust of 72 MPH. The winds blew down power poles, blew down a 100 foot radio tower, and car winds were broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong thunderstorms developed to the southeast of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the late afternoon and evening hours on September 6th. Some of the storms generated gusty outflow winds in excess of 50 mph which spread to the northwest and into the greater Phoenix area, causing dense blowing dust and dust storm conditions. The storms also generated damaging microburst winds and locally heavy rain, affecting locations such as San Tan Valley, San Tan Mountain Park and Seville. Later in the evening new thunderstorms developed across the northern portion of the Phoenix area, causing heavy rain and flooding in communities such as Peoria and prompting the issuance of a Flash Flood Warning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain developed across the northern portion of the greater Phoenix area during the early evening hours on September 6th, and some of the heaviest rain occurred in the community of Peoria. At 2043MST, a trained spotter 3 miles southeast of Peoria measured 1.65 inches within 45 minutes. The heavy rain led to significant street flooding in Peoria which resulted in the issuance of an Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisory. At 2115MST, a report was received from local broadcast media indicating that over one foot of flooding was occurring from 5100W to 5500W on Pinnacle Peak Road. The street flooding, located in an area about 4 miles east of Peoria, encompassed approximately a one half mile stretch of road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Steering flow shifted to a southeasterly direction as the upper high migrated to northeast Arizona, with precipitable water values between 1.25 and 1.50 inches. A morning storm caused flash flooding in Tucson. Afternoon storms created strong winds that produced a haboob along Interstate 10 in Pinal County. Fortunately, no traffic accidents occurred as a result of the dust storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of one half inch in less than 30 minutes over the Tucson area resulted in early morning flash flooding. A car was swept down the Alamo Wash at Calle Betelgeux near 22nd St. and Kolb Rd. Two occupants of the vehicle were rescued without injury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low was located off the central California coast. A short wave moved northeast across southeast Arizona and acted on very moist air consisting of 1.7 inches of precipitable water.  Thunderstorms produced very heavy rain and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred along and near the Babocamari River north of Huachuca City in the Presidential Estates area.  A woman and three dogs were rescued from a mini-van which was submerged in 3 feet of water on Bowers Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon storms fired once again, mainly concentrated in Pima County, in conjunction with a weak shortwave trough moving through southern Arizona. Storms produced torrential rain which resulted in road closures on the Tohono O'Odham Nation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred south of Gu Vo. Many roads including Indian Route 1 were impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level trough was positioned over the central U.S. with mid-level winds very weak suggesting slow storm movement. Deep moisture was still in place as precipitable water values ranged from 1.5 to 1.7 inches and surface dewpoints were solidly in the 60's. Another active thunderstorm day occurred with one fatality due to lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local law enforcement reported that a maintenance worker was struck by lightning while outside the middle school in Sells. The individual was flown to a Tucson area hospital and later succumbed to injuries sustained from the strike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced very heavy rainfall and vast quantities of hail up in an inch in diameter. The hail floated and piled several feet deep.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 2.60 inches of rain fell over the northeast side of Flagstaff in an hour and a half. Large quantities of hail also fell. Both the water and floating hail  caused flash flooding that impacted over 30 homes. Route 66 and the railroad in east Flagstaff were closed because of the water and hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low was located off the central California coast. A short wave moved northeast across southeast Arizona and acted on very moist air consisting of 1.7 inches of precipitable water.  Thunderstorms produced very heavy rain and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused significant street flooding on Tucson's east side. A woman was rescued from her sedan in flood waters 1 to 2 feet deep at Broadway Blvd and Camino Seco.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid level high pressure was centered over eastern Texas with a trough axis extending from southern Manitoba southwestward into northern California. Hurricane Norbert was located south of the tip of Baja California. Blended total precipitable water amounts were near 1.35 near the international border, with a weak disturbance departing southeast Arizona toward the north. Clear skies behind this disturbance allowed sufficient surface heating for showers and thunderstorms to initiate during the afternoon hours with heavy rain and flash flooding occurring near the International Border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding along Peck Canyon. Two males were rescued from Peck Canyon just outside of Rio Rico. Neither were injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid level high pressure was centered over eastern Texas with a trough axis extending from southern Manitoba southwestward into northern California. Hurricane Norbert was located south of the tip of Baja California. Blended total precipitable water amounts were near 1.35 near the international border, with a weak disturbance departing southeast Arizona toward the north. Clear skies behind this disturbance allowed sufficient surface heating for showers and thunderstorms to initiate during the afternoon hours with heavy rain and flash flooding occurring near the International Border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of over two inches in less than two hours flooded parts of Arivaca Road, causing it to be closed to traffic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tongue of monsoon moisture pushed into southern Mohave County, generating one severe thunderstorm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter driving on Highway 93 southeast of Wikieup estimated a 65 mph gust from a thunderstorm. He had to pull over and stop because his car was being blown off the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced very heavy rainfall and vast quantities of hail up in an inch in diameter. The hail floated and piled several feet deep.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced copious amounts of hail up to one inch in diameter. The hail,  without drifting, was up to three inches deep in places. Heavy rain...up to 2.60 inches...caused the hail to float and flow down streets. The hail drifted to over three feet deep in places.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced heavy rain and hail in Cottonwood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused street flooding in Cottonwood and one street (Aspen) was closed due to the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several strong thunderstorms developed across Yavapai County with heavy rain and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Agua Fria River near Humboldt jumped from around 1.9 feet to near 9.2 feet (a 1500 CFS increase) in a short time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain and flash flooding in the Agua Fria River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river gauge on the Agua Fria River near Humboldt showed a seven foot jump (close to 1,500 CFS).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a ridge of high pressure slid further to the east, strong thunderstorms hit the Tucson metro area. Rainfall amounts ranged from 1 to 2 inches with flash flooding in central to south Tucson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms brought heavy rainfall to the Tucson area. Rainfall rates of over 2 inches per hours and storm totals of between 1.5 inches and 2.0 inches resulted in flash flooding of the streets of central to south Tucson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved east and southeast off the Mogollon Rim and high terrain of western New Mexico, causing flash flooding in southern Greenlee and northern Cochise counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall that flooded five homes in Bowie, some with up to a foot of water. The courthouse also sustained minor damage from an inch of water entering the building. High water also caused the temporary closure of a short stretch of Interstate 10 from Mile Marker 365 to near 366. Multiple car accidents occurred in that area, forcing the closure of both the eastbound and westbound lanes of Interstate 10 for about an hour. Apache Pass and Roberts Farm Road were also closed in Bowie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak trough over the west coast focused moisture into eastern portions of southsern Arizona and the majority of monsoon storms developed over Cochise County. The strongest storms were along the international border where large hail occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter originally found quarter size hail, but later called back when he found pieces that were the size of half dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved east and southeast off the Mogollon Rim and high terrain of western New Mexico, causing flash flooding in southern Greenlee and northern Cochise counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three inches of rain in less than an hour caused flash flooding in Duncan.  One home was flooded with water up to 3 feet deep and damage was sustained to a car wash structure. Two to three feet of water cover part of Highway 70 on the northwest edge of Duncan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several strong thunderstorms developed across Yavapai County with heavy rain and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Road 15 washed out at mile marker 26 near Yava.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several strong thunderstorms developed across Yavapai County with heavy rain and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced 1.50 inches of rain in Bagdad in about 90 minutes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter measured 1.80 inches of rain in only one hour near the town of Dewey.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water, rocks, and mud flowed over several roads in Oatman. Waterfalls formed in the Black Mountains.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds knocked trees onto power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level trough continued to approach the area from the west. 12z precipitable water from the KTUS sounding was down to just over 1 inch, likely due to significant mid and upper-level drying. Winds veered from SSW at the surface to NE at 700 mb. Storms initially generated over the terrain with some moving off to the east and northeast over valley locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A high based, short-lived funnel cloud was seen over Babocamari Ranch around 1315 LST. Photo was received from the public via local media. The captain at the Sierra Vista Fire Department confirmed the funnel never touched the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A push of monsoon moisture fueled thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert. Some storms produced severe weather and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water covered Route 66 south of Golden Shores, and the road was eventually closed all the way south to I-40.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant push of monsoon moisture fueled an outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert. Many storms produced severe weather and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Oatman-Topock Highway was closed from mile markers 1 to 7 due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high amplitude ridge was centered over the western U.S. with surface high located in eastern New Mexico. Precipitable water values ranged from 1.25 to 2 inches, and a flash flood watch was in effect for the area.  Monsoon storms fired ahead of a cyclonic circulation which was located in northern Sonora. Numerous showers and thunderstorms moved north through southern Arizona, causing severe winds and flash flooding mainly in the Tucson area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media reports a portion of a roof was blown off of a residence on the 300 block of North Schrader Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced heavy rain over Williams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Williams reported 0.95 inches of rain in just 45 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Marie tracked west of Baja California and out to sea, but resulted in a southeast flow of moisture into southeast Arizona. Morning surface dew points were in the 60s to near 70, with precipitable water amounts of 1.5 to 1.8 inches. Pima and Pinal Counties received the majority of the heavy rainfall and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over 2 inches of rain caused significant flooding of route 286 about halfway between Three Points and Sasabe, near mile post 24. Roads were also flooded near and west of Arivaca.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major surge of monsoon moisture brought an outbreak of thunderstorms to the Mojave Desert and southern Great Basin, with the most activity on the 3rd and 4th. Widespread flash flooding and severe weather occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight to ten inches of flowing water, mud, and rocks washed across low spots on several roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching mid to upper level trough combined with elevated moisture from Hurricane Marie to the southwest led to numerous thunderstorms producing heavy rain and flash flooding. The 12Z KTUS sounding was nearly saturated and showed very weak flow through the column up to about 500 mb.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced over an inch of rain in less than 30 minutes in Tucson causing extensive street flooding in midtown. Some areas affected include Seneca and Country Club Roads, Grant Road between Swan and Campbell, as well as Tucson Boulevard between Speedway and Grant. A swift water rescue occurred at Fort Lowell Rd at Tucson Blvd for a vehicle stalled in flood waters. Water was up to 2 feet deep in some spots.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rocks and debris washed over many of the side roads in Colorado City, and also over low lying spots on Cane Beds Road.

Swift water rescue occurred late in the evening at this location when a woman drove into a wash and got stuck. At the time of the rescue, no barricades were up to indicate road had been closed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three cars ended up in the wash off Estrella Road, and a total of 13 people were rescued from vehicles around town. At least three people had to wait on top of their cars.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to two and a half feet of water flowed over Pierce Ferry Road at Archibald Wash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant push of monsoon moisture fueled an outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert. Many storms produced severe weather and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down in Bullhead City, including some onto cars and one onto a mobile home. Two power poles were broken and several power lines taken down, knocking out power to about 6000 customers. One roof was blown off, and some other buildings had minor roof damage. A light pole was knocked down, and a satellite dish was bent. The KIFP ASOS recorded a peak gust of 68 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The center of the upper level high moved eastward through northern Arizona, with a modest east-northeast flow over southern Arizona and a precipitable water value of 1.67 inches from the morning KTUS sounding. A few rounds of storms moved through southeastern Arizona, with the strongest storms occurring during the evening hours producing large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two spotters in Oracle reported a maximum hail size of one inch, mixed in with smaller pieces. Hail fell for about 20 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant push of monsoon moisture fueled an outbreak of thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert. Many storms produced severe weather and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Stockton Hill Road was closed from mile marker 16 to mile marker 40, and Pierce Ferry Road was closed at mile marker 16 due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant low and mid-level moisture was present under weak ridging with a number of triggering mechanisms which caused numerous showers and thunderstorms. Weak mid-level flow led to slow erratic movement of storms. Flash flooding and thunderstorm wind damage occurred in the Tucson area. A landspout tornado occurred near Willcox.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred on Sahuarita Road near Davidson Rd that was about 40 feet wide and 1 foot deep. Debris in the water consisted of small branches and rocks. Spotter expected, from a historical perspective, that flooding would be worse on Sahuarita Road further to the west.

Weak mid-level flow led to slow erratic movement of storms. Flash flooding and thunderstorm wind damage occurred in the Tucson area. A landspout tornado occurred near Willcox.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a tree at Broadway Blvd. and Harrison Rd. A porch roof was blown off a house a mile west on Broadway at Camino Seco.  A palm tree was also downed at that location. In between, near Bluefield St. and Porter Ave, a 50 foot pine tree was downed and part of a roof was blown off a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An initial band of moisture associated with Tropical Depression Odile, once rated as strong as a Category 4 hurricane, contributed to heavy rainfall in Greenlee County on September 16th. A precipitable water value of 1.91 inches was noted on the Tucson sounding at 1200 UTC September 17th. Odile took a sharp turn to the east later that day, which limited rainfall from Tucson westward, although widespread heavy rain fell in Cochise, Santa Cruz and southeast Graham Counties. From the afternoon of September 17th into the early morning hours of September 18th, rainfall amounts of 1 to 3 inches were common with locally heavier amounts of 4 to 6 inches reported. Multiple reports of flooding were received due to both flash flooding and river flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple road and bridge closures occurred in response to heavy rainfall across much of Cochise County. Increased flows on the San Pedro River eventually over-topped both the Highway 92 bridge in Palominas and the Hereford Bridge, causing both to be closed for a period of time. The Hereford Bridge sustained damage, and it did not re-open until nearly a month later on October 16, 2014. Further east, rainfall in the Old Bisbee area weakened a portion of a hillside. Falling soil and rock collapsed a retaining wall which fell onto a vehicle, while another residence sustained damage. The bridge at Cave Creek Road near Portal which leads to Cathedral Rock cabins was completely washed out. Water rushed around several buildings up to 2 feet deep in some locations but little damage occurred. One resident was evacuated from her home as a precaution. Additionally, many roads in rural Cochise County were closed for a time due to areal flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another push of monsoon moisture fueled thunderstorms over the Mojave Desert. Several storms produced flash flooding, and isolated large hail also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: London Bridge Road was impassable due to flooding from a rapidly flowing wash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough of low pressure was located off of the northern California coastline with ridge axis extending from Arizona northeastward into Minnesota. A weak disturbance near the base of the trough moved across southeast Arizona. Ample moisture was available with a precipitable water of 1.53 from the morning KTWC sounding, and moderate instability with a lifted index of -5 and surface-based CAPE close to 2000 J/kg. Severe thunderstorms with heavy rain resulted in a range of storm reports including a funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm south of Patagonia produced a brief funnel cloud. Multiple reports and pictures were received both from the public and broadcast media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Simon, once rated a Category 4 hurricane with maximum sustained winds of 135 mph, was the fourth tropical system of 2014 to bring rainfall to southeastern Arizona. An upper level disturbance located over the southwestern United States helped to advect copious amounts of moisture into the area ahead of Simon, which eventually produced heavy rainfall and flooding on October 8 and 9. Numerous roads on the Tohono O'Odham Nation were closed for more than 24 hours due to flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain in excess of 2 on the Tohono O'Odham Nation caused several road closures for an extended period of time. Highway 86 became impassable in several locations between mile markers 76 and 106. One motorist was rescued from flood waters near mile post 79 after attempting to cross them with their vehicle.  High flows on the Hickiwan Wash near its confluence with the San Simon Wash near Highway 86 were exacerbated by the compromise of several earthen berms designed to protect the highway. High flows along the San Simon Wash continued downstream from there to the confluence with the Vamori Wash near Santa Cruz, where Indian Route 21 was impassable, then south to the International Border. High flows occurred on the Santa Rosa Wash from Highway 86 downstream into Pinal County causing the closure of Indian Route 15 at North Komelik. Water levels finally receded during the evening hours of October 9th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tucson Electric & Power reported that thunderstorm winds downed several power poles and lines in an area bounded by River Road, Swan Road, Fort Lowell Road and Alvernon Way. About 1100 customers lost power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The center of high pressure was located over western Texas with a weak upper low over central California. Surface dewpoints were in the low to middle 60's with a precipitable water of 1.50 on the 12z KTWC sounding. Light south to southwesterly flow prevailed over southeastern Arizona. Monsoon thunderstorms occurred, raining heavily over areas mainly south and east of Tucson, where several inches of rain had just fallen recently in association with the remnants of Odile. Additionally, the heavy rains of 4 to 6��� which occurred near headwaters of the San Pedro River in Mexico on Sept. 17, also from Odile, caused elevated flows along the San Pedro River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain which occurred near the headwaters of the San Pedro River in Mexico and also over Cochise County due to Tropical Depression Odile caused significant rises on the San Pedro River. Water eventually made its way north through Mammoth, where the river overflowed it banks. Three homes and rural roads were flooded. Other residences were evacuated as a precaution. Residents were allowed back in their homes in the morning as the water receded. No injuries occurred.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from the remnants of Odile previously saturated soils in Cochise County. Monsoon thunderstorms then brought another round of heavy rain to the Bisbee area, where a concrete retaining wall along a wash collapsed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure aloft located over the Pacific Ocean near 25N/135W brought a period of well above normal temperatures to southeastern Arizona. Tucson International Airport reached a high of 89 degrees F, or about six degrees above normal. A 7-month old girl lost her life after being left in a car for an extended period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 7-month old girl succumbed to her injuries after she was left in the back seat of a car. Her father realized she was unconscious only after he had been driving, and called 911 at 1720 LST. She was taken to Arizona Medical Center where she was pronounced dead a short time later. It is not clear how long the baby was in the car, but it may have been as long as 8 hours. The high temperature was 89 degrees with a relative humidity of 26%.

Southerly wind ahead of the storm brought deep moisture ahead of the front setting the stage for severe thunderstorms and heavy rain. Rainfall from this storm were as high as 2.50 inches in 24-36 hours. Little Colorado River near Winslow peaked at about 7 feet below the railing of the bridge on Interstate 40.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain fell for 10 to 15 minutes with mostly pea sized hail. Some hail was 1/2 inch in diameter. The heavy rain flooded the yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a low-amplitude ridge in place over the Great Basin and surface high pressure located over northern Arizona, an initial surge of tropical moisture ahead of then Tropical Storm Odile helped prime the atmosphere for showers and thunderstorms. One particularly strong storm developed while moving southwestward off of the Mogollon Rim late in the evening, with thunderstorm wind damage occurring in Pinal County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds damaged 6 warehouses at the cotton-storage facility STS Land Holding in Eloy. Four warehouses were listed as a 30 to 50 percent loss. The other 2 buildings were still marginally usable. The warehouses, covered in tin, were mostly empty at the time. A few minutes later, 39 out of 107 cars on a Union Pacific train were derailed near the Interstate 10 Picacho exit. The freight containers were empty at the time. No one was injured. Extensive damage to the tracks also resulted causing the rail line to remain out of service until repairs were completed, three days later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture associated with Tropical Depression Norbert, once rated as strong as a Category 3 hurricane, contributed to very heavy rainfall across southeast Arizona during the morning and early afternoon hours of September 8th.  Precipitable water values of 2.03 inches were noted on the 12z KTUS sounding. Rainfall amounts on September 8th totaled 1.5-2.5��� over a large portion of northeast Pima and southern Pinal Counties. Amounts of 2.5 to 4.5��� of rain fell along western portions of the Santa Catalina Mountains and adjacent areas of the Catalina Foothills, Casas Adobes, Oro Valley, Catalina and Oracle. The rainfall total at the Tucson International Airport for the 8th was 1.84���, a calendar day record. Flash flooding in and around the Tucson Metro area was extensive with numerous swift water rescues occurring. Two separate instances of motorists driving into water which was rapidly flowing across roadways resulted in two fatalities. Larger river channels experienced high flows through the afternoon and evening, including the Rillito River, Ca��ada del Oro Wash and Santa Cruz River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from Norbert extended into the early afternoon hours. Several additional  swift water rescues occurred. One occurred in the Pima Wash on Rudasill Rd between 1st Avenue and Oracle Road, where a single occupant was successfully rescued after his vehicle was swept off the road by quickly flowing water. Around the same time but further north, a swift water rescue occurred near Hardy Rd and Northern Ave in Oro Valley. Yet another rescue occurred when a 69-year old man and his 76-year old wife got caught in flash flood waters near Highway 79 in Oracle Junction. The couple attempted to drive through flood waters near Suizo Road when their Honda Accord began floating down Cadillac Wash. They both exited the car and the man made it to dry land, but his wife was regrettably swept down the wash and perished as she was unable to swim.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought (D3) conditions continued across far eastern Pinal and much of western Graham Counties during October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought (D3) category persisted across far eastern Pinal and western Graham Counties through October. While there were small signs of improvement, even bountiful rainfall was insufficient to significantly ease drought conditions. Ranchers continued to be affected by the prior depletion of rangeland forage vegetation. Precipitation during the month ranged from 75 to 150 percent of normal, and soil moisture increased slightly, to nearly 80 percent of normal. Levels at San Carlos Reservoir rose slightly to about 7 percent of capacity by the end of October, the highest level since early May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A back door cold front moved through southeast Arizona on November 5th. A tight pressure gradient caused strong east winds to funnel through high elevation passes in parts of the Huachuca and Santa Rita Mountains.  Wind gusts over 65 mph were measured for several hours as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hopkins RAWS, located at 7100 feet in a saddle area in the Santa Rita Mountains, measured sustained east winds at 40 to 60 mph and gusts of 65 to 75 mph during the overnight hours of November 5th.  A peak gust of 88 mph was measured at 12:19 am MST on November 6th.

Multiple reports of flooding were received due to both flash flooding and river flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Graham County Sheriff's Office reported 4 homes in Solomon area flooded between 1650 and 1710 LST when a wash came out of its banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought strong winds to much of the Mojave Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust occurred at Bullhead City (KIFP).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An initial band of moisture associated with Tropical Depression Odile, once rated as strong as a Category 4 hurricane, contributed to heavy rainfall in Greenlee County on September 16th. A precipitable water value of 1.91 inches was noted on the Tucson sounding at 1200 UTC September 17th. Odile took a sharp turn to the east later that day, which limited rainfall from Tucson westward, although widespread heavy rain fell in Cochise, Santa Cruz and southeast Graham Counties. From the afternoon of September 17th into the early morning hours of September 18th, rainfall amounts of 1 to 3 inches were common with locally heavier amounts of 4 to 6 inches reported. Multiple reports of flooding were received due to both flash flooding and river flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Graham County dispatch reported that Ash Creek was flowing rapidly which resulted in the closure of Cluff Ranch Rd. Eventually, Ash Creek came out of its banks and began running into a residential area in the town of Pima. At least 1 home was flooded, and 6 people were air lifted out of Ash Canyon due to loss of road access and flooding concerns. No one was injured.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding in Nogales occurred late in the evening as Ephraim Wash flooded over Washington Ave, with 2 homes flooded and several families evacuated off of Western Ave around 2200 LST. Nogales Wash also overtopped its banks from Doe St. downstream to Chula Vista Lane. In addition, concrete slabs within the wash were damaged, leaving the International Outfall Interceptor (IOI) that carries 9-12 million gallons of raw sewage from Sonora, Mexico to Rio Rico, exposed. In Rio Rico, Sonoita Creek flooded Pendleton Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions returned to parts of southeast Arizona during the latter half of February.  For the water year beginning in October of 2013, most areas only received 25 to 50 percent of normal precipitation through February. There was only one significant precipitation event during that time period, which occurred in November.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought (D2) conditions present over much of southeast Arizona deteriorated into the extreme drought (D3) category in eastern Pinal, western Graham, extreme northeastern Pima and much of northern Cochise counties by mid February. These  counties were among those listed as primary natural disaster areas by the USDA. The growing season had not yet started, so vegetation impacts were minimal, but soil moisture conditions were generally less than 30 percent of normal and San Carlos Reservoir was a less than 14 percent of capacity. Fuel dryness levels were drier than the long term average for February and significantly drier than in February 2013.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching storm system produced strong winds and blowing dust across areas of northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at Winslow reported wind gusts in excess of 58 MPH for close to two hours. The peak gusts were 69 MPH. Blowing dust was also reported. The ADOT wind sensor at Jackrabbit reported a peak wind gust of 58 MPH at 239 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching storm system produced strong winds and blowing dust across areas of northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blowing dust reduced the visibility to 1000 feet about halfway between Teec Nos Pos and Kayenta including the Dennehotso area (Mile post 410 on Highway 160). The wind gusts were estimated around 40 MPH with the visibility down to around a mile for much of the afternoon and less than 1/4 mile at times in the community of Many Farms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought locally high winds to the Mojave Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This gust occurred at Pipe Spring National Monument.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Breezy southwest winds developed across south central Arizona during the afternoon hours on April 30th in association with an area of low pressure which developed across the desert southwest. In addition to the breezy conditions, the atmosphere was sunny, dry and unstable. The conditions were favorable for the formation of dust devils. A dust devil developed in Cave Creek during the early afternoon hours, and the associated strong winds damaged the roof of a local nursery. No injuries were reported due to the roof damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the early afternoon hours on April 30th, a strong dust devil developed approximately 3 miles south of Cave Creek. As reported by local broadcast media, KTVK Channel 3 TV,  gusty winds estimated to be in excess of 45 mph damaged the roof of the Desert Foothills Gardens Nursery. An aluminum roof panel on the top of the roof was lifted and tossed nearly 30 feet by the dust devil. The weight of the panel was estimated to be 600 pounds, and the dimensions were approximately 55 by 35 feet. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moving across the desert southwest generated gusty west winds across the central Arizona deserts during the afternoon and evening hours on April 26th. Wind gusts generally ranged from 30 to 40 mph, although there were a few higher gusts. The winds were sufficient to warrant the issuance of a wind advisory for the lower deserts including the greater Phoenix metropolitan area. Some of the stronger wind gusts produced isolated tree damage in the Phoenix area, mostly in the form of downed large branches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper level low pressure system moving through the desert southwest generated gusty west winds across the central Arizona deserts during the afternoon and evening hours on April 26th. Some of the wind gusts were in excess of 40 mph, and were sufficient to cause isolated instances of tree damage across the greater Phoenix area. At 1400 MST, a trained spotter in Gilbert reported 5 to 6 inch diameter tree branches were blown down near the intersection of Riggs and Higley road. Another trained spotter in Papago Park reported a couple of tree branches blown down near Thomas Road and 60th street. The diameter of the branches was estimated to be greater than 2 inches. This occurred at 1430 MST. A wind advisory was issued for the central Arizona deserts, including the greater Phoenix area, from 800 MST through 1700 MST, and it was in effect when the tree damage occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A human caused fire started out just north of Slide Rock State Park on May 20 just before 4 PM during Red Flag Conditions. The fire quickly spread north up the canyon and northwest up the side of the canyon. A type I Incident Management Team was called in to fight the fire. In all, over 22,000 acres burned. Most of that was low to moderate intensity. At the peak, over 1,200 personnel were involved in bringing this fire under control.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A human caused fire started less than 1/4 mile north of Slide Rock State Park at around 350 PM on May 20 during Red Flag Conditions. The fire quickly spread north and eventually out of the canyon and into the edge of the Western Mogollon Rim. Fire burned over 22,000 acres beginning about 6 miles north of Sedona in Oak Creek Canyon, West Fork Oak Creek Canyon, and parts of the rim country bordering the canyons. The fire stayed west of Oak Creek and Highway 89A that connects Sedona and Flagstaff. The total fire fighting cost was over $7 million. The cost of rehabilitation and the expected flood damage has yet to be determined.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper level low pressure system moved out of the eastern Pacific and into Arizona on the first weekend of March, bringing locally heavy rainfall and isolated severe thunderstorms to south central Arizona. During the day on Saturday, March 1st, widespread moderate to heavy rain fell; many locations across the central deserts including the Greater Phoenix area picked up amounts in excess of one inch. The rain led to flooding of area roads and washes and prompted the issuance of several Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisories. In addition, isolated severe thunderstorms developed, affecting portions of the greater Phoenix area. The storms produced tree damage, generated small to moderate hail and even produced a weak tornado which briefly touched down in east Mesa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system moved slowly east across Arizona on Saturday March 1st, and it led to the formation of isolated severe thunderstorms across the greater Phoenix area. Due to strong wind shear and modest instability, a small EF0 tornado developed in east Mesa. The tornado had a very short track, less than one mile, and touched down near Extension road and Main Street in the Palm Cove apartment complex. The tornado knocked down a number of large trees, ripped spanish roofing tiles off of a number of apartment units, and sent an empty hot tub flying 1300 feet across the apartment complex. Several eyewitnesses saw the tornado and the flying hot tub. Fortunately, no injuries were reported as a result of the tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed across south central Arizona during the afternoon hours on March 25th. The storms generated gusty outflow winds in excess of 40 mph which spread across portions of southwest Maricopa and northern Pinal counties. Due to the very dry conditions, the gusty winds were able to pick up significant amounts of dust and dirt, and this led to localized dust storm conditions across the deserts. A Dust Storm Warning was issued for south central Arizona which included the southern part of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours across portions of northern Pinal county, and they produced gusty outflow winds in excess of 30 mph. The gusty winds picked up significant amounts of dust and generated dust storm conditions in the area around Casa Grande. A trained weather spotter located 2 miles north of Arizola reported visibility below one quarter of a mile in dense blowing dust at 1527 MST. Winds were gusting to around 30 mph at the time of the report. The dense blowing dust led to hazardous driving conditions during the afternoon rush hour, although no accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and moist storm system brought heavy rain and higher elevation heavy snow to northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across the Eastern Mogollon Rim.  The rain changed to snow in the  afternoon...and by midnight...over 8 inches of snow fell. Forest Lakes reported a total of a foot of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire burned 100 acres and threatened several homes near Paulden. Dry and windy conditions helped fan the flames.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rancher was burning weeds and the fire escaped due to windy and abnormally dry conditions. The fire grew to 100 acres and threatened several homes.  The Prescott Airport just 20 miles away had only received 0.02 inches of rain in the prior 7 weeks.  Average amount for this time period is over 2.50 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions continued in parts of southeast Arizona through March.  Other than one day of significant precipitation over parts of the drought area on March 1st, the month was generally dry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought (D3) category persisted in eastern Pinal, western Graham, extreme northeastern Pima and much of northern Cochise counties through March.  One precipitation event brought up to 150% of normal monthly precipitation in one day over parts of southern Graham and northern Cochise counties on March 1st, but the remainder of the month was dry. Soil moisture conditions were generally less than 30 percent of normal and San Carlos Reservoir was at less than 11 percent of capacity. Fuel moisture levels remained very low causing wildfire concerns.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm impacted Southern Arizona on the first day of March. A strong low pressure system off the California coast opened up and dropped through the base of the trough over the southwest U.S.  Interaction of this system with an atmospheric river from the Pacific helped push precipitable water values above the 99th percentile for March. Strong winds along the backside of a line of strong showers caused some wind damage, while the second round of rainfall caused flash flooding in Pima and Santa Cruz Counties. The mountain ranges north and east of Tucson received several inches of snow above 7000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall occurred on the higher peaks of the Pinaleno Mountains. 12 to 24 of snowfall occurred on Mt. Graham at elevations between 9000 and 10,000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high amplitude ridge over the West Coast and a strong trough over the eastern United States created a very strong gradient over northern Arizona. The winds were channeled through a mountain pass and created very strong winds on Agassiz Peak just north of Flagstaff at 11,500 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very strong northeast winds hit Agassiz Peak just north of Flagstaff. A weather station at 11,500 feet recorded wind gusts in excess of 58 MPH for almost 12 hours. The highest wind gust recorded was 80 MPH.

The mountain ranges north and east of Tucson received several inches of snow above 7000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A non-thunderstorm wind gust of 58 mph was measured at Ryan Airfield. Around the same time, shingles were blown off of a roof about 3 miles to the east north east of Ryan Airfield, with sustained winds of 37 mph (measured) gusting to approximately 55 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain was determined to be the cause of a 1-vehicle accident involving two women at Kilometer 15 on 1-19 in Rio Rico. The car drifted to the left into the center median at approximately 1215 MST and struck a tree. One woman was killed and the other was seriously injured.

A strong low pressure system off the California coast opened up and dropped through the base of the trough over the southwest U.S.  Interaction of this system with an atmospheric river from the Pacific helped push precipitable water values above the 99th percentile for March. Strong winds along the backside of a line of strong showers caused some wind damage, while the second round of rainfall caused flash flooding in Pima and Santa Cruz Counties. The mountain ranges north and east of Tucson received several inches of snow above 7000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds estimated at about 50 mph brought down a 30-foot tall saguaro on top of a carport at a residence in Green Valley. One of the cactus arms rolled into the street and another landed against a neighboring house. Heavy rain of more than one inch during the previous 24 hours likely loosened up the soil enough that the wind blew the cactus over. There was extensive damage to the carport, and 3 vehicles, including a golf cart, an SUV, and a vintage Cadillac.

Law enforcement reported fast moving water that was 35 feet across flowing near Forest Service Road 4084. There was also a report of several people stranded on the other side of this flowing water in Gardner Canyon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed across the central deserts during the late evening hours on July 13th, and they produced gusty outflow winds in excess of 50 mph. Despite heavy rains across the area earlier in the evening, the winds were still able to pick up dust and generate dust storm conditions which affected portions of the greater Phoenix area, mainly the southeastern communities including Sun Lakes and Seville. Strong winds moved into the northern portion of the greater Phoenix area and flipped a full sized trampoline. No accidents of injuries were reported with this dust storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across the central deserts during the late evening hours, and they produced gusty outflow winds in excess of 50 mph. Despite locally heavy rains earlier in the evening, the winds were still able to pick up significant amounts of dust and create dust storm conditions which affected the southeast portions of the greater Phoenix area. At 2145MST, a trained spotter located 4 miles east of Sun Lakes reported a dust storm, with visibility down to just one tenth of a mile. Additionally, he estimated wind gusts of 45 to 55 mph. A second trained spotter 4 miles west of Seville reported a dust storm at approximately 2200MST. Due to the late evening time frame, few people were likely driving and a such no accidents or injuries were reported due to the dust storm. A Dust Storm Warning was not issued for this event, rather a Blowing Dust Advisory was in effect from 2144MST until 2303MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed across the central deserts during the late evening hours on July 13th, and they produced gusty outflow winds in excess of 50 mph. Despite heavy rains across the area earlier in the evening, the winds were still able to pick up dust and generate dust storm conditions which affected portions of the greater Phoenix area, mainly the southeastern communities including Sun Lakes and Seville. Strong winds moved into the northern portion of the greater Phoenix area and flipped a full sized trampoline. No accidents of injuries were reported with this dust storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed to the southeast of the greater Phoenix area during the late evening hours on July 13th, and they generated strong gusty outflow winds which quickly spread to the north and moved into Deer Valley and north Scottsdale. According to a public report, at 2215MST, gusty winds estimated to be at least 80 mph lifted and spun around a full sized backyard trampoline. The trampoline, which was being held down by 50 pound sandbags, ended up wedged under the roof between the house and a yard wall. The home was located at the intersection of Cave Creek Road and Pinnacle Peak Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the late evening hours across the greater Phoenix metropolitan area on July 26th. The stronger storms generated gusty and damaging microburst winds in excess of 60 mph which uprooted trees and blew down power poles and power lines. Some of the most significant damage occurred over far northern portions of the greater Phoenix area, including Desert Ridge Marketplace, Deer Valley and Peoria. In addition to the strong gusty winds, locally heavy rain occurred which led to urban flooding and the issuance of Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisories. Finally, the strong winds briefly led to dust storm conditions over central portions of the Phoenix area, including Tumbleweed Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with strong damaging microburst winds developed across the northern portion of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the late evening hours on July 26th, affecting communities such as Deer Valley and Scottsdale. The powerful outflow winds, estimated to be at least 70 mph at times, created significant damaged to trees and power poles in the area near Desert Ridge Marketplace. A meteorologist reported that huge trees were blown down at an apartment complex located at Greenway Road and 40th Street. The trees crushed several cars at the complex. Power poles and lines were also downed by the strong winds. At about the same time, a trained spotter reported numerous large trees downed near Greenway and 44th street. Finally, according to local Fire and Rescue, more trees and power lines were blown down on Greenway Road between Tatum and 41st street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the late evening hours across the greater Phoenix metropolitan area on July 26th. The stronger storms generated gusty and damaging microburst winds in excess of 60 mph which uprooted trees and blew down power poles and power lines. Some of the most significant damage occurred over far northern portions of the greater Phoenix area, including Desert Ridge Marketplace, Deer Valley and Peoria. In addition to the strong gusty winds, locally heavy rain occurred which led to urban flooding and the issuance of Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisories. Finally, the strong winds briefly led to dust storm conditions over central portions of the Phoenix area, including Tumbleweed Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense thunderstorms developed across the northern portion of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the late evening hours on July 26th. In addition to damaging microburst winds, the stronger storms produced locally heavy rainfall with peak rain rates approaching 2 inches per hour. At 2334MST, a trained weather spotter about 3 miles southwest of Desert Ridge Marketplace measured 1.15 inches of rain in the span of 31 minutes. The rain led to minor urban flooding and resulted in the issuance of an Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisory at 2319MST. The advisory continued through the early morning hours on July 27, expiring at 0115MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure was located near the Las Vegas, NV area on the morning of April 26, 2014. As the low moved through northern Arizona, strong and gusty southwest winds were experienced in parts of southeastern portions of the state in response to the tightening pressure gradient and subsequent passing of a cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Southwest winds gusting to between 60 and 70 mph were common during the afternoon hours in Santa Cruz and Cochise counties. The observatory at Mt. Hopkins in the Santa Rita Mountains reported several gusts as high as 66 mph. The KALK and FKHU ASOS at Fort Huachuca and the ASOS at Bisbee-Douglas Airport all measured  maximum gusts from 58 to 61 mph between 1300 and 1430 MST. The strong winds fanned a small 75 acre wildfire west of Sierra Vista. The human-caused Aerostat fire was attended to quickly and contained by 1900 MST that evening. No injuries or damage were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an active overnight period in Cochise County, another round of storms fired off to the south and east of Tucson during the afternoon and evening hours. Precipitable water values remained between 1.5 and 1.7 under southeasterly mid-level flow, with an inverted trough located across Eastern Chihuahua. Upwards of 0.75 of rain fell near the International Border over areas which were already saturated from the previous day, causing flash flooding in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Back to back days of heavy rainfall caused major flash flooding in downtown Sierra Vista, with rainfall amounts of just over 1 in Hereford to 2.49 in Sierra vista. 2,000 cfs of flow was measured at the Charleston Bridge on the San Pedro River. Moson Road and Hereford Road both had to be barricaded because they were impassable due to flooding according to local law enforcement. Also, there were newspaper reports that a taxi took on flood waters on 7th street just north of Willcox Drive. The main gate of Fort Huachuca was closed from 2030 on Friday until 0015 on Saturday due to flooding in the area. Traffic had to be re-routed to the east gate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface high pressure migrated slightly to the northeast and was positioned over southwest Colorado. An inverted trough moved through southeast Arizona from the east, inducing easterly flow. Precipitable water amounts were near 1.6, and a third consecutive day of heavy rain over Cochise County resulting in further flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain across Cochise County resulted in further flooding in parts of Sierra Vista. Early in the day, normally dry washes were still running from the previous day's rainfall, and storm debris (mainly rocks) remained on Willcox Drive and Buffalo Soldier Trail. After storms rolled through during the evening hours, law enforcement reported Moson Road closed yet again, this time at mile post 5, and Kings Ranch Road near Highway 92 was flooded over. The storm also washed out the only access for residents living along South Paloma Trail in Palominas, stranding several families.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms moved southeast from west central Arizona into western and central Pima County during the early morning hours. These storms produced very heavy rain and caused flash flooding on the Tohono O'odham Nation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding of 30 homes near Menager's Dam and two in Gunsight. Water was up to one foot deep. Gunsight Wash overflowed its banks and washed out Indian Road 5 near Gunsight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across the south central deserts during the afternoon hours on July 13th, and they primarily affected the greater Phoenix metropolitan area. Several of the storms produced microburst winds in excess of 60 mph, damaging trees, signs and buildings across the greater Phoenix area. The storms prompted the issuance of many Severe Thunderstorm Warnings. In addition, the storms produced locally heavy rains with rainfall rates in excess of 2 inches per hour. The rain led to widespread urban flooding, and prompted the issuance of several Urban and Small Stream Flood Advisories as well as Flash Flood Warnings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across the greater Phoenix area during the late afternoon hours on July 13th, and some of the stronger storms produced gusty microburst winds. According to a broadcast media report, a wind gust of 59 mph was measured in Higley, just to the west of Phoenix Mesa Gateway airport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms with microburst winds developed across portions of the greater Phoenix area during the late evening hours on July 26th. Some of the storms affected Luke Air Force Base. The AWOS weather sensor at Luke measured a wind gust of 60 mph.

A trained weather spotter just to the southwest of Deer Valley Municipal Airport reported that numerous power poles were blown down near the intersection of 7th Avenue and Deer Valley Road. Wind gusts were estimated to be close to 70 mph. Additionally, another trained spotter a bit further south, about 3 miles north of North Mountain Park reported a number of trees blown down. Street flooding was also occurring in the same location.

According to a trained weather spotter 2 miles northeast of Peoria, thunderstorm winds estimated to be near 65 mph blew down a large tree near the intersection of 67th Avenue and Pinnacle Peak Road. The downed tree was blocking traffic. No injuries were reported due to the downed tree.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense thunderstorms developed across the greater Phoenix area during the late evening hours on July 26th, and they produced gusty outflow winds in excess of 50 mph which primarily affected northern and central portions of the Phoenix area. The gusty winds created dust storm conditions and led to the issuance of a Dust Storm Warning at 2223MST. The warning expired at 2300MST, and was replaced by a Blowing Dust Advisory which ran through midnight. At 2204MST, a trained spotter in east Mesa reported 40 mph winds and indicated visibility was below one half mile and dropping. At 2219MST, another trained spotter 2 miles northwest of Seville reported a dust storm with one quarter mile visibility. He also reported winds gusting to 50 mph. Finally, at 2229MST, a trained spotter 4 miles southeast of Tumbleweed Park reported a dust storm. Fortunately, no injuries or accidents were reported due to the dust storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across portions of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the evening hours on August 1st, and the stronger storms produced locally heavy rainfall. According to trained weather spotters, peak rain rates were measured in excess of 1.5 inches per hour and this rainfall was sufficient to create episodes of urban and small stream flooding in addition to flash flooding. Some of the more significant flooding occurred near North Mountain Park, and necessitated a water rescue. No injuries or accidents were reported due to the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across the northern portions of the greater Phoenix area during the evening hours on August 1st and they affected communities such Glendale, Deer Valley and North Mountain as well as the Interstate 17 corridor. The stronger storms produced locally heavy rainfall with rain rates well in excess of one inch per hour. At 2141MST, a trained weather spotter 1 mile west of Metrocenter Mall in North Mountain measured 0.75 inches of rain within 45 minutes. He reported urban flooding with water curb to curb and running a few feet into some of the yards in the area. Additionally, at 2146MST another trained spotter 2 miles north of Glendale measured 1.36 inches of rain within 45 minutes. The heavy rain resulted in the issuance of multiple Urban and Small Stream Advisories for the area, which remained in effect into the early morning hours on August 2nd.

Some of the strongest storms of the evening occurred in the area around Desert Ridge Marketplace. A trained weather spotter reported estimated peak wind gusts to 69 mph. The gusts produced significant damage in the area as well as knocking out power.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with damaging microburst winds developed across the northern portions of the greater Phoenix metropolitan area during the late evening hours on July 26th. The storms affected communities such as Deer Valley, Paradise Valley and Scottsdale. A trained weather spotter in Deer Valley, about 4 miles west of Desert Ridge Marketplace, reported trees uprooted near the intersection of 16th Street and Utopia. Peak wind gusts were estimated to be near 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late morning hours on July 15th, thunderstorms developed across portions of south central Arizona, including the communities of Queen Creek and Superior. The storms produced locally heavy rainfall, with rainfall rates between 1 and 2 inches per hour. The rain led to urban and small stream flooding, which included water 6 inches deep flooding the main street in the town of Superior. Additionally, there was increased flow in Queen Creek with water running 6 inches deep. No injuries were reported due to the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain developed in the area around Superior during the early afternoon hours on July 15th. The storms were slow moving, and produced rainfall rates in excess of one inch per hour, which led to local flooding of streams, washes and area roads. A Small Stream Flood Advisory was issued for Superior, starting at 1313MST and continuing until 1615MST. At 1325MST a trained weather spotter 1 mile northeast of Superior measured 0.94 inches of rain which fell in less than one hour. Shortly thereafter, the spotter reported that water was 6 inches deep across the main street in Superior, and Queen Creek was flowing with water about 6 inches deep. At 1342MST, a second trained spotter in Superior reported street flooding in Superior and on Highway 60 just east of Superior. Flow continued in Queen Creek through the late afternoon hours, and the depth of flowing water exceeded 12 inches by 1645MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitable water values in Southeast Arizona were between 1.5 and 1.8 inches with weak steering flow. Thunderstorms that developed were very slow moving with rainfall amounts in excess of one inch in some locations. Flash flooding occurred in both Pima and Greenlee Counties. Heavy rains in Nogales, Sonora caused flooding in Nogales, AZ which closed the Deconcini Port of Entry for the first time in history.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds uprooted a tree at Three Points.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A metal three-sided hay barn was flattened by thunderstorm winds at an exotic bird sanctuary in Cascabel, AZ.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought (D3) conditions persisted in parts of southeast Arizona through May as little if any rain was observed. The drought area remained unchanged from April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought (D3) category persisted across eastern Pinal, western Graham, extreme northeastern Pima and much of northern Cochise counties through May. Virtually no rain fell in the drought area.  Levels at San Carlos Reservoir fell to about 6 percent of capacity by the end of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 77 about 3.7 miles north of Mammoth, AZ was closed at mile marker 117 due to flash flooding. Rainfall in excess of 0.75 inches fell in a short period of time with a slow-moving thunderstorm, causing the road to flood. The same storm produced a storm total of 3.5 inches in Dudleyville, AZ.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitable water values in the 1.3-1.5 inch range combined with weak steering flow produced a few very slow moving and heavy raining thunderstorms. Some storms impacted the Tucson metro, with wind damage, power outages, flash flooding and swift water rescues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds snapped six power poles and downed trees along Old Spanish Trail from Irvington to Golf Links Roads. Over 1600 homes were left without power southeast of Houghton Escalante Roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One man died of heat related causes in the Lake Mead National Recreation Area. His companion is still missing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One man died of heat related causes while hiking to Arizona Hot Spring. A woman believed to have accompanied him on the hike is still missing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon on July 8th, a line of intense thunderstorms developed across central La Paz county; the stronger storms produced damaging microburst winds as well as very heavy rainfall which led to flash flooding. Some of the most significant damage occurred in Quartzsite; several RV properties were heavily damaged there. Later in the afternoon, strong thunderstorms developed in the Parker area, causing several mobile homes to be turned over.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of intense thunderstorms developed across central La Paz county during the middle of the afternoon on July 8th and they moved slowly towards the west, affecting the community of Quartzsite. The storms produced damaging microburst winds, estimated to be in excess of 70 mph. The storms prompted the issuance of s Severe Thunderstorm Warning for the Quartzsite area, which was in effect from 1605MST until 1745MST. According to the Quartzsite Fire and Rescue, the storms heavily damaged a number of RV properties in La Paz Valley as well as areas approximately 6 miles to the south of Quartzsite. A number of thunderstorm damage photos were posted to the Quartzsite Fire Twitter feed during the late afternoon hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon on July 8th, a line of intense thunderstorms developed across central La Paz county; the stronger storms produced damaging microburst winds as well as very heavy rainfall which led to flash flooding. Some of the most significant damage occurred in Quartzsite; several RV properties were heavily damaged there. Later in the afternoon, strong thunderstorms developed in the Parker area, causing several mobile homes to be turned over.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense thunderstorms developed across portions of northwest La Paz county during the late afternoon hours on July 8th, and they affected the town of Parker. The strong storms produced damaging microburst outflow winds estimated to be in excess of 80 mph. According to local law enforcement, 4 mobile homes were overturned in Parker. In addition, the gusty winds blew down power poles and caused roof damage to local residences. Fortunately, no injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface high pressure was centered over southeastern Colorado with a ridge over the western U.S. The morning KTUS sounding indicated 1.5 of precipitable water, mid-level easterly flow of 15-25 kts, and steep mid-level lapse rates. Monsoon storms formed over the higher terrain and eventually moved into valley locations, including the Tucson metro. Flash flooding and damaging winds were reported in Pima County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain in the Tucson area caused flash flooding on the southeast side of town. Flash flooding was reported I-10 and Wilmot Rd. and at Kolb and Irvington Roads where water was 1-2 feet deep. Broadcast media reported that numerous cars got stuck in flowing water. Tucson Fire Department was called to rescue one adult and one child, who climbed a tree to escape quickly-flowing waters in a wash near Escalante Rd. and Camino Seco Blvd. No one was injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface high pressure was centered over southeastern Colorado with a ridge over the western U.S. The morning KTUS sounding indicated 1.5 of precipitable water, mid-level easterly flow of 15-25 kts, and steep mid-level lapse rates. Monsoon storms formed over the higher terrain and eventually moved into valley locations, including the Tucson metro. Flash flooding and damaging winds were reported in Pima County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed multiple trees, power poles and lines on Tucson's east side on and around Davis-Monthan AFB, leaving over 10,000 customers without power. Two trees landed on vehicles. One tree near Stella and Wilmot fell on a house, causing major damage. Several power poles were downed near Benson Highway and Campbell Ave. Five telephone poles were snapped near Irvington and Pantano Roads and roofs were blown off two houses near Escalante and Pantano Roads. A retired, 40-thousand pound F-16 was flipped over on top of another retired jet at the Davis-Monthan AFB boneyard. Strong winds punctured holes in the sun shades in a few of the aircraft shelters. Operational aircraft did not sustain any damage. The ASOS on base a mile away from the boneyard measured a peak gust of 66 mph.

Some of the most significant damage occurred in Quartzsite; several RV properties were heavily damaged there. Later in the afternoon, strong thunderstorms developed in the Parker area, causing several mobile homes to be turned over.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of intense thunderstorms developed across central La Paz county during the afternoon hours and they affected the community of Quartzsite. The stronger storms produced gusty, damaging microburst outflow winds. According to Quartzsite Fire and Rescue, a wind gust was measured at 61 mph in the town of Quartzsite.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon on July 8th, a line of intense thunderstorms developed across central La Paz county; the stronger storms produced damaging microburst winds as well as very heavy rainfall which led to flash flooding. Some of the most significant damage occurred in Quartzsite; several RV properties were heavily damaged there. Later in the afternoon, strong thunderstorms developed in the Parker area, causing several mobile homes to be turned over.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense thunderstorms developed in the Quartzsite area during the late afternoon hours on July 8th and they produced very heavy rainfall. Radar indicated that rainfall rates were in excess of 2 inches per hour. The heavy rain led to flash flooding in Quartzsite, especially the eastern and southern parts of town. A Flash Flood Warning was in effect from 1700MST until 1930MST. Flash flooding was evident on a number of pictures posted on the Quartzsite Fire Twitter feed; area washes were running full and overflowing and vehicles were stranded in the middle of flowing washes, necessitating swift water rescues. Fortunately no injuries were reported due to the flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions persisted in parts of southeast Arizona through April. Meager precipitation amounts caused the area of extreme (D3) drought to expand farther northward in Graham County and westward in Pinal County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought (D3) category persisted and actually expanded slightly in eastern Pinal, western Graham, extreme northeastern Pima and much of northern Cochise counties through April. Two meager precipitation events occurred on the 19th and 26th of the month but only managed to produce between 10 and 50 percent of the monthly average rainfall in the majority of the drought area. These two light rain events did produce lightning strikes that sparked a number of wildfires, including the Skunk Fire in northwest Graham County. Soil moisture conditions were generally less than 20 percent of normal and San Carlos Reservoir was at less than 9 percent of capacity.

The storms produced gusty outflow winds in excess of 40 mph which spread across the central deserts and affected communities such as Casa Grande and Sun Lakes. The strong winds generated dense blowing dust which prompted the issuance of a Dust Storm Warning. In addition, they also damaged trees in the community of Sun Lakes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with strong and gusty outflow winds developed across Pinal county during the evening hours on July 8th. The winds, estimated to be in excess of 40 mph, generated dust storm conditions across the central deserts, mainly southeast of the greater Phoenix area. They were also strong enough to cause tree damage. According to a broadcast media report, at 2330MST in Sun Lakes, gusty winds uprooted a 35 foot tree which then fell on a house. Additionally, the winds caused damage to a several roofs in Sun Lakes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across the central deserts during the late afternoon hours on July 25th, and due to favorable atmospheric conditions, they were able to generate strong and gusty outflow winds in excess of 40 mph. The outflow winds spread across much of the central deserts, including the greater Phoenix area, and generated dust storm conditions. A number of weather spotters reported visibilities at or below one quarter mile in dense blowing dust, and Dust Storm Warnings as well as Blowing Dust Advisories were issued. No injuries were reported due to the dense blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across the central deserts during the late afternoon hours on July 25th, and due to favorable atmospheric conditions, were able to generate strong gusty outflow winds in excess of 40 mph. The gusty winds picked up significant amounts of dust and caused dust storm conditions; a Dust Storm Warning was issued for the greater Phoenix metropolitan area from 1645MST until 1900MST. At 1718MST, a trained spotter 4 miles southwest of Firebird Lake reported visibility down to 150 feet near State Route 347 and Riggs Road due to dense blowing dust. At 1735MST, a National Weather Service employee 3 miles east of Superstition Springs reported half mile visibility at the intersection of Baseline and Ellsworth roads. Finally, at 1747MST, the AWOS automatic weather station at the Falcon Field airport measured one quarter mile visibility in blowing dust. Although the dense blowing dust caused very hazardous driving conditions during the evening rush hour, no accidents or injuries were reported due to the dust storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure centered in southern California helped to bring moisture northward from Sonora, Mexico into Southeastern Arizona. Showers and thunderstorms occurred throughout the day over areas mainly to the south and east of Tucson, with one thunderstorm producing quarter size hail near Douglas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 1 inch hail which accompanied a thunderstorm moving through the Douglas area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure was located near the Las Vegas, NV area on the morning of April 26, 2014. As the low moved through northern Arizona, strong and gusty southwest winds were experienced in parts of southeastern portions of the state in response to the tightening pressure gradient and subsequent passing of a cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Southwest winds gusting to between 60 and 70 mph were common during the afternoon hours in Santa Cruz and Cochise counties. The observatory at Mt. Hopkins in the Santa Rita Mountains reported several gusts as high as 66 mph. The KALK and FKHU ASOS at Fort Huachuca and the ASOS at Bisbee-Douglas Airport all measured  maximum gusts from 58 to 61 mph between 1300 and 1430 MST. The strong winds fanned a small 75 acre wildfire west of Sierra Vista. The human-caused Aerostat fire was attended to quickly and contained by 1900 MST that evening. No injuries or damage were reported.
